perturbation.original_text: Definitions: (a) “Confidential Information ” means the information relating to the Purpose provided by one Party to the other Party and clearly marked “CONFIDENTIAL” or if related orally or visually, identified as “CONFIDENTIAL” at the time of disclosure. Confidential Information includes, but is not limited to, formulations, know -how, manufacturing processes, inventions, products, processes, techniques, compositions, compounds, plans, practices, drawings, prototypes, recordings, instructions, manuals, papers or other materials in whatever form or nature;
perturbation.changed_text: Definitions: (a) “Confidential Information” means the information relating to the Purpose provided by one Party to the other Party, regardless of whether it is marked “CONFIDENTIAL” or identified as such at the time of disclosure. Confidential Information includes, but is not limited to, formulations, know-how, manufacturing processes, inventions, products, processes, techniques, compositions, compounds, plans, practices, drawings, prototypes, recordings, instructions, manuals, papers or other materials in whatever form or nature;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for information to be marked or identified as "CONFIDENTIAL" to be considered confidential, the contract broadens the scope of what is protected, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that often require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_citation: Trade Secrets Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. T.20, s. 1
perturbation.law_explanation: Ontario trade secret law implies a requirement for active measures to protect confidentiality. By defining Confidential Information as anything related to the Purpose, even if unmarked, the modified contract contradicts the need for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially undermining trade secret protection under Ontario law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the requirement for it to be marked or identified as such. The Law Explanation indicates this contradicts the need for 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' under Ontario trade secret law, potentially undermining trade secret protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 primarily confirms access to Ontario's statutes, the Perturbation Explanation's emphasis on 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' supports the assessment that the modified contract could weaken such protections, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient recognizes that improper use of the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder shall cause irreparable damage to the Provider and agrees that the Provider may take any and all available legal action and shall be entitled to injunctive relief to prevent breaches of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient recognizes that improper use of the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder may cause damage to the Provider and agrees that the Provider may take legal action, however, injunctive relief is not guaranteed.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that injunctive relief is not guaranteed, it weakens the provider's ability to protect their confidential information. In cases where monetary damages are insufficient, injunctive relief might be the only viable option. This weakens the agreement and could cause enforcement uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Availability of Injunctive Relief in Ontario
perturbation.law_citation: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s. 101
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Courts of Justice Act doesn't explicitly guarantee injunctive relief, it establishes the court's power to grant it. By stating it's not guaranteed, the modified text could be interpreted as an attempt to contractually limit the remedies available to the Provider, which may not be enforceable and creates uncertainty regarding the court's inherent jurisdiction.

perturbation.justification: The original contract guarantees injunctive relief to the provider, while the changed text states that injunctive relief is not guaranteed. The Law Explanation states that the modified text could be interpreted as an attempt to contractually limit the remedies available to the Provider, which may not be enforceable and creates uncertainty regarding the court's inherent jurisdiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Courts of Justice Act, implying that the court has the power to grant injunctive relief. The modification weakens the provider's ability to protect confidential information, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario and the laws of Canada applicable therein and herby submit to the juris diction of the courts of the Provi nce of Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of Quebec and the laws of Canada applicable therein and herby submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Ontario.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law is now Quebec law while the jurisdiction remains in Ontario. This creates conflict because the Ontario courts will have to interpret Quebec law. Quebec's legal system is based on civil law, unlike Ontario's common law system, which can create issues in interpretation and application.
perturbation.law_citation: Rules regarding choice of law in contract disputes
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally agree on a choice of law, the choice must be reasonable and not contrary to public policy. Having Quebec law govern a contract adjudicated in Ontario courts introduces complexities and potential conflicts, as Ontario courts may be less familiar with Quebec's civil law system. This choice might be challenged if it significantly disadvantages one party or undermines the fairness of the proceedings.

perturbation.justification: The modified text presents a contradiction because it stipulates that Quebec law governs the agreement, while simultaneously submitting to the jurisdiction of Ontario courts. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the conflict arising from Ontario courts interpreting Quebec law, given the distinct legal systems (common law vs. civil law). The Law Explanation further supports this, suggesting that such a choice of law might be challenged due to potential unfairness and complexity. Although there are no useful scraped law snippets, the explanations provided establish that a legal contradiction exists due to the misalignment of governing law and jurisdiction, creating a potential challenge to the contract's enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information ” means the information relating to the Purpose provided by one Party to the other Party and clearly marked “CONFIDENTIAL” or if related orally or visually, identified as “CONFIDENTIAL” at the time of disclosure. Confidential Information includes, but is not limited to, formulations, know -how, manufacturing processes, inventions, products, processes, techniques, compositions, compounds, plans, practices, drawings, prototypes, recordings, instructions, manuals, papers or other materials in whatever form or nature;
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means any information the Provider subjectively believes is confidential, regardless of whether it relates to the Purpose or is marked “CONFIDENTIAL”. Confidential Information includes, but is not limited to, formulations, know-how, manufacturing processes, inventions, products, processes, techniques, compositions, compounds, plans, practices, drawings, prototypes, recordings, instructions, manuals, papers or other materials in whatever form or nature;
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of confidential information to be based on the provider's subjective belief and removing the connection to the defined 'Purpose' broadens the scope significantly and introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles of Contract Interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: Canadian contract law requires that contracts be interpreted based on the objective intentions of the parties and the surrounding circumstances. Defining 'Confidential Information' solely based on the Provider's subjective belief, regardless of its relevance to the Purpose or any objective indication of confidentiality, undermines this principle. A court might find this definition unreasonable and unenforceable, as it lacks objective criteria and could lead to arbitrary enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts legal principles of contract interpretation by introducing a subjective standard for defining 'Confidential Information'. While the provided snippets are empty, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation highlight that Canadian contract law requires objective intention. The modified definition, based solely on the Provider's subjective belief, deviates from this principle, potentially leading to arbitrary enforcement and unenforceability, thus creating a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to protect the Provider’s Confidential Information using at le ast the same degree of care Recipient uses to protect its own Confidential Information, but in no event less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to protect the Provider’s Confidential Information to the best of their ability, considering their current resources.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specified a minimum standard of 'reasonable care' and a comparison to the care used for the Recipient's own information. The modified text replaces this with a subjective 'best of their ability, considering their current resources,' which could allow for a lower standard of care depending on the Recipient's circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Care in Contract Law
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Negligence in Contractual Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: Even in contractual relationships, a general duty of care applies. The modified clause could be interpreted as attempting to contractually limit the Recipient's duty of care to a standard below what is considered reasonable, based on their resources. A court may find this limitation unenforceable if it undermines the fundamental purpose of the confidentiality agreement and falls below the necessary standard to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: duty of care | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you duty of care The duty of care is a fiduciary duty requiring directors and officers of a corporation to make decisions that pursue the corporation’s interests with reasonable diligence and prudence. This fiduciary duty is owed by directors and officers to the corporation, not the corporation’s stakeholders or broader society. The American Law Institute’s Principles of Corporate Governance defines the duty of care as the duty by which a corporate director or officer is required to perform their functions in good faith ; in a manner that they reasonably believe to be in the best interests of the corporation; and with the care that an ordinarily prudent person would reasonably be expected to exercise in a like position and under similar circumstances. Courts analyze the duty of care by applying the business judgment rule and examining the processes by which the directors and officers made decisions. Generally, courts do not subject these decisions to judicial review so long as the decision constitutes a valid business judgment. A valid business judgment is one that is made by financially disinterested directors and officers who have become duly informed before exercising judgment, and who exercise judgment in a good-faith effort to advance corporate interests that a reasonably prudent person would make. If the court finds bad faith , gross negligence , or bad processes, the court will subject the directors’ decision to judicial review to analyze whether there was a breach of duty of care. Generally, there is a different standard of negligence for directors and officers for many reasons. Courts want directors and officers to use their expertise to take risks and exercise discretion without the fear of liability, and courts do not want to conduct judicial review of every business decision. Moreover, directors possess greater expertise regarding business decisions than courts. And, it is difficult to objectively review decisions beforehand when the court has already seen its consequences in the present. Limiting exposure to the duty of care: Corporations can limit their exposure to the duty of care through indemnification , directors and officers insurance (D&O insurance), and waivers of liability . Indemnification is explicitly allowed by some corporate statutes . Indemnification authorizes corporations to reimburse any agent , employee, director, or officer for reasonable expenses for losses of any sort arising from any actual or threatened judicial proceeding or investigation so long as the losses result from actions undertaken on behalf of the corporation in good faith and do not arise from a criminal conviction . D&O insurance allows corporations to insure fiduciaries for anything (including crime and bad faith). Generally, however, the insurance market will limit what is actually covered (i.e. criminal activity) and typically only covers losses stemming from good faith decisions. Waivers of liability can limit and/or extend the Board of Directors ’ liability through the corporation’s charter. A corporate charter can extend and/or limit personal liability for director fiduciary duty unless it pertains to a breach of duty of loyalty ; acts or omissions in bad faith; intentional misconduct /violation of law; or any transaction from which a director has received an improper personal benefit. [Last reviewed in January of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords llcs-corporations-partnerships Wex COMMERCE business law corporations wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a minimum standard of 'reasonable care' or the same level of care the recipient uses for their own confidential information. The modified text replaces this with 'best of their ability, considering their current resources,' potentially allowing a significantly lower standard. This contradicts the general duty of care, as the 'Law Explanation' clarifies, potentially falling below a reasonable standard necessary to maintain secrecy. Snippet 1 defines 'duty of care' as requiring directors and officers to make decisions with 'reasonable diligence and prudence'. While this snippet discusses corporate governance, the underlying principle of reasonable diligence relates to the confidentiality agreement, and the modified text potentially undermines this principle.
